_meta.description: Curated WordPress plugin CVE registry. Consulted by the plugin_version_disclosure evidence detector to escalate version-fingerprint candidates into bug-class-correct candidates with severity matching the upstream CVE. Sourced from Wordfence Intelligence + NVD; intentionally a small curated subset (not the full WF feed) — full integration is M9+ work tracked at issue #32.
_meta.source: Wordfence Intelligence v3 production feed + NVD CVE detail

entries.summary: Unrestricted file upload via filename type bypass. An attacker can upload a file with a double-extension trick (e.g. ``shell.php.jpg``) that the plugin accepts but the webserver may execute as PHP.
Pre-auth admin-ajax.php action allows arbitrary plugin upload. An unauthenticated attacker can install a malicious plugin → RCE.
Unauthenticated SQL injection via the ``query_params`` parameter on the REST endpoint ``/wp-json/wp-statistics/v2/metabox``. Attacker-controlled input is concatenated into the SQL query.

Authentication bypass via the ``X-WCPAY-Platform-Checkout-User`` HTTP header. Setting the header to a target user-id (e.g. admin) impersonates that user on REST requests, granting full admin actions to an unauthenticated attacker.
Privilege escalation via REST API: type-juggling in the permission check at ``/wp-json/aioseo/v1/objects`` allows unauthenticated callers to invoke admin-only endpoints.
Account takeover via password-reset flow. A flaw in token validation lets an unauthenticated attacker reset any user's password, including admin.
Privilege escalation: the registration flow allowed setting arbitrary metadata including ``wp_capabilities``, granting administrator role to any registering user.
Broken access control on the ``wpforms_get_form_field_options`` AJAX action. Authenticated subscriber-level users can read form field configurations they should not have access to.
Unauthenticated SQL injection via the cache-clearing endpoint. Allows extraction of arbitrary database content including password hashes.
Unauthenticated read of backup archives via predictable filename pattern under ``/wp-content/backups-dup-pro/``. Backups contain wp-config.php with DB credentials and auth salts.
Unauthenticated code injection via the ``ninja_forms_ajax_submit`` handler. Attacker-controlled input reaches an ``unserialize()`` call → PHP object injection → RCE chain.
Authenticated SQL injection in the events search endpoint. Available to subscriber-level users and above.
Reflected XSS in admin context via the field-export endpoint. Requires admin to click a crafted link; result is admin-account compromise via cookie theft or CSRF setup.
Subscriber-level read of admin-only configuration via the indexable_objects endpoint. Information disclosure of the site's content-tree structure to authenticated low-privilege users.
Alias for the same Yoast SEO bug under the alternate slug. Subscriber-level info disclosure of indexable_objects.
Stored XSS via cache-tag injection on the admin dashboard. Unauthenticated request can poison the cache with attacker-controlled HTML; admin loading the dashboard executes the script.
Authentication bypass on 2FA flow. The ``skip_onboarding`` REST endpoint allowed bypassing two-factor verification entirely.
Privilege escalation via the ``/wp-json/jetpack/v4/contact-form/`` endpoint. Authenticated low-privilege users could submit forms as administrators in some configurations.
Order-edit screen leaks customer addresses to shop-manager-level users for orders they didn't process.
Server-side request forgery via the URL-validation path on redirect rule creation. Authenticated admin can be tricked into proxy-fetching internal URLs.
